title: Velocity & Speed
subtitle: Velocity vs speed in Class 11 Physics — why direction matters

blocks.heading: The Idea
blocks.body:
Speed is how fast you are covering ground — it is a scalar (no direction).
Velocity is speed with direction — it is a vector. A car going north at 60 km/h and a car going south at 60 km/h have the same speed but opposite velocities.
Average values smooth out everything over a time interval. Instantaneous velocity (or speed) is the exact value at one frozen moment — the number your speedometer shows right now.

blocks.heading: Average velocity:
blocks.body:
Average speed:
Instantaneous velocity (the limiting case as the time interval shrinks to zero):
Symbols:
\bar{v} — average velocity (m/s); can be negative
\bar{s} — average speed (m/s); always ≥ 0
\Delta x — displacement (m)
\Delta t — time interval (s)
v — instantaneous velocity (m/s)

blocks.heading: Real-World Example
blocks.body: Your car's speedometer reads instantaneous speed — the speed at this exact moment. When Google Maps estimates your arrival time, it uses your average speed for the whole journey. If you brake hard for a speed bump then floor it again, the speedometer fluctuates wildly but Maps only cares about total distance ÷ total time.

blocks.heading: Try It At Home
blocks.body: Walk from one wall to the opposite wall of a room and time yourself. Then walk back. Calculate (a) average speed for the whole trip, and (b) average velocity for the whole trip. Why does one come out zero?

blocks.heading: What If…
blocks.body: Can an object have a non-zero speed but zero velocity? (Hint: think of a planet completing one full orbit — what is its displacement after one year?)

quiz.question: A ball is thrown straight up and comes back to the thrower's hand after 4 s. The average velocity over the full trip is:
options: Equal to half the maximum speed
quiz.explanation: The ball starts and ends at the same point, so displacement = 0. Average velocity = 0 ÷ 4 = 0. Average speed would be non-zero (total distance ÷ time).
